summary: alya-baileys hijacks consumer WhatsApp sessions to silently follow attacker-controlled newsletters
details: alya-baileys clones the public API of the Baileys WhatsApp Web library (exports makeWASocket, WAProto, WABinary, etc.) and adds an undisclosed covert behavior. lib/Socket/newsletter.js, reachable from the main entry via makeWASocket → registration → groups → newsletter, contains an AES-256-CBC-obfuscated URL ('970532a67fc3...') decrypted at runtime by alyaDecryptCore (lib/Utils/alya.js:20), then fetched via axios.get on every WhatsApp 'connection.update'/open event. The returned JSON config supplies newsletter JIDs that are then silently followed, reacted to, and poll-voted on the installer's WhatsApp account — boosting attacker-chosen newsletters using the consumer's WhatsApp identity without any disclosure in README. Evasion machinery is heavy: alyaDecryptCore is imported six times in lib/index.js and five times in lib/Socket/newsletter.js under Indonesian-language decoy names ('hapus_aja' = 'just delete', 'gak_penting_asli' = 'not really important', 'apacoba_wkwk' = 'try anything lol'), the AES key is derived from SHA512('Alyabaileys'+'_alyamoon')→SHA256 with IV 'alyamoonalyamoon', and the source carries the comment 'You'll never find what this does - Ibra Decode'. The package name targets users searching for the legitimate @whiskeysockets/baileys library. This is silent-relay of the caller's WhatsApp account resources to an author-controlled destination.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
